questions.question: What is the purpose of the CIS Kubernetes Benchmark?
options: To define minimum hardware requirements for Kubernetes clusters
To provide security configuration guidelines for hardening Kubernetes components
To benchmark cluster performance under load
To certify Kubernetes distributions for production use
questions.explanation: The CIS (Center for Internet Security) Kubernetes Benchmark provides prescriptive security configuration guidelines for hardening Kubernetes components including the API server, etcd, controller manager, scheduler, and worker nodes. It defines specific settings and their expected values for a secure configuration.

questions.question: Which tool automates CIS Kubernetes Benchmark checks against a running cluster?
options: kubectl audit
kube-bench
kube-hunter
kubelet
questions.explanation: kube-bench is an open-source tool that runs the CIS Kubernetes Benchmark checks against a cluster. It tests the configuration of the API server, etcd, kubelet, and other components, reporting which checks pass, fail, or require manual review.

questions.question: A kube-bench scan reports that the API server has --anonymous-auth=true. What is the security concern?
options: Anonymous users can modify cluster resources
Unauthenticated requests are accepted by the API server, potentially exposing cluster information
All API requests bypass RBAC authorization
Anonymous auth enables automatic secret decryption
questions.explanation: With anonymous-auth enabled, the API server accepts unauthenticated requests as the 'system:anonymous' user. While RBAC still applies, default ClusterRoles might grant read access to certain resources. CIS recommends disabling anonymous auth or ensuring no RBAC rules grant permissions to anonymous users.

questions.question: Which API server flag ensures that all incoming connections use TLS encryption?
options: --enable-admission-plugins
--tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file
--authorization-mode=RBAC
--audit-log-path
questions.explanation: The --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file flags configure the API server to serve HTTPS using the specified certificate and key. Without TLS, API communication is transmitted in plaintext, exposing tokens, secrets, and configuration data to network-level attacks.

questions.question: How should the Kubernetes Dashboard be secured in a production environment?
options: Expose it via a NodePort Service for easy access
Grant it cluster-admin privileges so administrators can manage everything
Use token-based authentication, restrict RBAC permissions, and never expose it publicly
Disable HTTPS to simplify the configuration
questions.explanation: The Kubernetes Dashboard should use token-based authentication (not skip login), have minimal RBAC permissions (not cluster-admin), and should never be exposed to the internet. Access should be through kubectl proxy or a VPN. Compromised dashboards with excessive permissions have led to cryptojacking incidents.

questions.question: An Ingress resource exposes an application at 'app.example.com'. Which security controls should be configured on the Ingress?
options: TLS termination with a valid certificate, rate limiting, and web application firewall rules
Only a NodePort backend; TLS is unnecessary for internal apps
Anonymous authentication to simplify user access
Disable HTTP to HTTPS redirects for backward compatibility
questions.explanation: Production Ingress resources should terminate TLS with valid certificates, enforce HTTPS redirects (no plaintext HTTP), and leverage Ingress controller features like rate limiting and WAF rules. Annotations vary by controller (e.g., NGINX Ingress supports ssl-redirect, rate-limit, and ModSecurity WAF).

questions.question: A NetworkPolicy is applied to restrict traffic between namespaces. However, DNS resolution stops working for affected Pods. What is the likely cause?
options: NetworkPolicies always block DNS traffic
The egress policy does not include a rule allowing traffic to the kube-dns/CoreDNS Pods on port 53
DNS does not work with NetworkPolicies enabled
The Pods need a special DNS annotation to work with NetworkPolicies
questions.explanation: When a NetworkPolicy restricts egress traffic, DNS queries to CoreDNS (typically in the kube-system namespace on UDP/TCP port 53) are also blocked unless explicitly allowed. Egress rules must include a namespaceSelector or podSelector allowing traffic to the DNS service Pods on port 53.

questions.question: kube-bench reports that the etcd server does not have --client-cert-auth=true. What is the risk?
options: etcd data is not encrypted at rest
Clients can connect to etcd without presenting a valid client certificate, potentially accessing all cluster secrets
etcd cannot replicate data to other nodes
The API server cannot write to etcd
questions.explanation: Without --client-cert-auth=true, etcd accepts connections from any client, even without a valid certificate. Since etcd stores all Kubernetes state including Secrets, an attacker with network access to etcd port 2379 could read or modify any cluster data. Client certificate authentication ensures only authorized clients (like the API server) can connect.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a default-deny NetworkPolicy?
options: To block all traffic to and from the Kubernetes API server
To deny all ingress and/or egress traffic to Pods unless explicitly allowed by other policies
To prevent new Pods from being created in the namespace
To disable the CNI plugin for the namespace
questions.explanation: A default-deny NetworkPolicy blocks all ingress, egress, or both for all Pods in a namespace. Additional NetworkPolicies then selectively allow only the required traffic. This follows the zero-trust principle where no traffic is permitted by default and must be explicitly authorized.

questions.question: The CIS Benchmark recommends setting --profiling=false on the API server. Why?
options: Profiling reduces API server performance
Profiling endpoints can expose sensitive internal details about the API server to attackers
Profiling is incompatible with RBAC
Profiling data is written to etcd, consuming storage
questions.explanation: Profiling endpoints expose internal performance data and code paths of the API server. An attacker could use this information to identify performance bottlenecks to exploit for denial of service or to understand internal architecture for targeted attacks. Disabling profiling reduces the attack surface.

questions.question: Which verification endpoint is commonly used to check the health of the Kubernetes API server?
options: /api/v1/status
/healthz
/readyz
Both /healthz and /readyz
questions.explanation: /healthz checks general API server health, while /readyz checks if the server is ready to serve traffic. Both endpoints should return 200 OK. /livez was added for liveness checks. These endpoints should not require authentication in production but should not be exposed publicly.

questions.question: You need to restrict egress traffic from Pods in the 'web' namespace to only allow HTTPS (port 443) and DNS (port 53). Which NetworkPolicy achieves this?
options: A policy with egress rules for port 443 TCP and port 53 UDP/TCP, with podSelector: {}
A policy with ingress rules for ports 443 and 53
A policy with egress rules for all ports to 0.0.0.0/0
A policy blocking all ingress and allowing all egress
questions.explanation: An empty podSelector selects all Pods in the namespace. Egress rules specifying port 443 (TCP) and port 53 (both UDP and TCP for DNS) with policyTypes: ['Egress'] restrict outbound traffic to only HTTPS and DNS. All other egress traffic (SSH, HTTP, etc.) is denied.

questions.question: Which API server flag controls the authorization mode and should include RBAC for production clusters?
options: --enable-admission-plugins
--authorization-mode
--authentication-mode
--client-ca-file
questions.explanation: The --authorization-mode flag specifies which authorization modules are used (e.g., RBAC, Node, Webhook, ABAC). Production clusters should use --authorization-mode=Node,RBAC. The Node authorizer handles kubelet requests, and RBAC provides fine-grained access control for users and service accounts.

questions.question: What is the security risk of exposing the kubelet read-only port (10255)?
options: It allows writing arbitrary files to the node filesystem
It exposes Pod specs, container info, and node metrics without authentication
It enables remote code execution on the node
It allows modifying kubelet configuration
questions.explanation: The kubelet read-only port (10255) serves metrics and Pod information without any authentication. An attacker with network access can enumerate running Pods, read container specs (including environment variables), and gather node metrics. CIS recommends setting --read-only-port=0 to disable it.

questions.question: How often should CIS Benchmark checks be run against a Kubernetes cluster?
options: Only during initial cluster setup
Regularly (e.g., after upgrades, configuration changes, and on a periodic schedule)
Only when a security incident occurs
Once per year during the annual audit
questions.explanation: CIS Benchmark checks should be run regularly, not just during initial setup. Configuration drift, upgrades, and operational changes can introduce security regressions. Automated scheduled scans (e.g., via CI/CD or cron jobs running kube-bench) catch misconfigurations before they are exploited.

questions.question: What is the purpose of RBAC in Kubernetes?
options: To encrypt communication between Pods
To control who can perform what actions on which resources in the cluster
To manage container image repositories
To schedule Pods on specific nodes
questions.explanation: RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) regulates access to Kubernetes resources based on the roles assigned to users, groups, or ServiceAccounts. It defines what verbs (get, list, create, delete, etc.) a subject can perform on which API resources (pods, secrets, deployments, etc.).

questions.question: What is the difference between a Role and a ClusterRole in RBAC?
options: Roles are for users; ClusterRoles are for ServiceAccounts
Roles grant permissions within a single namespace; ClusterRoles grant permissions cluster-wide or across namespaces
Roles are more secure than ClusterRoles
There is no functional difference; they are interchangeable
questions.explanation: A Role defines permissions within a specific namespace. A ClusterRole defines permissions cluster-wide and can access non-namespaced resources (nodes, PersistentVolumes) or be used across multiple namespaces via RoleBindings. ClusterRoles should be granted sparingly because they affect the entire cluster.

questions.question: A developer needs read-only access to Pods in the 'staging' namespace only. Which combination of RBAC resources should be created?
options: ClusterRole with get/list on Pods + ClusterRoleBinding
Role with get/list on Pods in 'staging' namespace + RoleBinding in 'staging' namespace
ClusterRole with get/list on Pods + RoleBinding in 'staging' namespace
Both B and C are correct approaches
questions.explanation: Both approaches work. You can create a namespace-scoped Role with a RoleBinding (option B), or reference a ClusterRole in a namespace-scoped RoleBinding (option C). The latter is useful when the same permissions are needed across multiple namespaces -- define the ClusterRole once and create RoleBindings in each namespace.

questions.question: An RBAC audit reveals a ClusterRoleBinding granting 'cluster-admin' to a ServiceAccount in the 'default' namespace. Why is this a critical security risk?
options: The default namespace should not have ServiceAccounts
cluster-admin grants unrestricted access to all resources; any Pod using this ServiceAccount has full cluster control
ClusterRoleBindings cannot reference ServiceAccounts
The cluster-admin role is deprecated
questions.explanation: cluster-admin grants unrestricted access to all Kubernetes resources. If a Pod uses this ServiceAccount (either explicitly or by being in the default namespace where this SA is auto-mounted), a container escape or pod compromise gives the attacker complete cluster control -- including creating privileged pods on any node.

questions.question: Why should you set 'automountServiceAccountToken: false' on ServiceAccounts that do not need API access?
options: To improve Pod startup performance
To prevent the ServiceAccount token from being available inside the container, reducing the impact of container compromise
To disable network access for the Pod
To prevent the Pod from using persistent volumes
questions.explanation: When a ServiceAccount token is mounted in a Pod, a compromised container can use it to authenticate with the Kubernetes API. Setting automountServiceAccountToken: false prevents this, reducing the blast radius of a container compromise to only the container itself, not the Kubernetes API.

questions.question: What is a bound ServiceAccount token and how does it improve security over legacy tokens?
options: It is encrypted with the cluster's CA key
It is audience-bound, time-limited, and Pod-bound, reducing the window of exploitation if leaked
It uses a longer key length for stronger authentication
It is stored in an external secret manager instead of etcd
questions.explanation: Bound ServiceAccount tokens (introduced in Kubernetes 1.20+) are projected tokens that are audience-bound (only valid for a specific API server), time-limited (auto-expire), and object-bound (invalidated when the Pod is deleted). Legacy tokens were long-lived and remained valid even after the Pod was deleted.

questions.question: How do you restrict which nodes a kubelet can access using the Node authorization mode?
options: The Node authorizer automatically restricts kubelets to only access resources related to Pods scheduled on their node
You must manually configure a NetworkPolicy for each node
The kubelet is restricted by its ServiceAccount's RBAC Role
Node authorization is not related to kubelet access control
questions.explanation: The Node authorization mode (--authorization-mode=Node) restricts kubelet API access to only the resources needed for Pods running on that specific node. A kubelet can only read Secrets, ConfigMaps, and PersistentVolumeClaims referenced by Pods on its node, preventing a compromised kubelet from accessing resources on other nodes.

questions.question: An attacker compromises a Pod and retrieves the auto-mounted ServiceAccount token. The token has permissions to create Pods. How can the attacker escalate privileges?
options: The attacker cannot escalate because Pods are isolated
The attacker can create a privileged Pod that mounts the host filesystem and access node-level resources
The attacker can only read logs from other Pods
The token is useless outside the original Pod
questions.explanation: With Pod creation permissions, an attacker can create a privileged Pod with hostPID, hostNetwork, and hostPath volumes, effectively gaining root access to the underlying node. This is why Pod creation should be tightly controlled, and admission controllers should prevent privileged Pods.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the NodeRestriction admission controller?
options: To prevent kubelets from modifying their own node labels with specific prefixes reserved for administrators
To restrict the number of Pods per node
To prevent nodes from joining the cluster without approval
To limit CPU usage per node
questions.explanation: The NodeRestriction admission controller prevents kubelets from modifying node labels with the node-restriction.kubernetes.io/ prefix and certain other labels reserved for administrators. It also limits kubelets to only modify their own Node object and the Pods bound to their node, preventing a compromised node from affecting the broader cluster.

questions.question: You discover a RoleBinding that grants the 'edit' ClusterRole to the 'system:authenticated' group in a namespace. What is the risk?
options: Only cluster administrators can use the edit role
Any authenticated user (including all ServiceAccounts) can create, modify, and delete most resources in that namespace
The edit role only allows read access
The system:authenticated group includes only human users
questions.explanation: The system:authenticated group includes ALL authenticated users AND all ServiceAccounts. The 'edit' ClusterRole grants create, update, delete on most namespace resources. This means any Pod's ServiceAccount in any namespace can modify resources in this namespace -- a massive exposure.

questions.question: What is the recommended approach for upgrading a Kubernetes cluster securely?
options: Skip minor versions to save time
Upgrade one minor version at a time, review release notes for security fixes and deprecations
Only upgrade when a critical CVE is announced
Upgrade all components simultaneously to minimize downtime
questions.explanation: Kubernetes supports upgrading one minor version at a time (e.g., 1.28 to 1.29). Skipping versions is not supported and may break the cluster. Release notes should be reviewed for security fixes, deprecated APIs, and changed defaults that could affect security posture.

questions.question: A security audit finds ServiceAccounts with Secrets still using legacy (non-expiring) tokens. What remediation is recommended?
options: Delete all ServiceAccounts and recreate them
Migrate to projected bound tokens, delete unused legacy token Secrets, and ensure no applications depend on the old token format
Rotate the cluster CA certificate
Disable all ServiceAccount authentication
questions.explanation: Legacy ServiceAccount tokens are stored as Secrets and never expire, posing a risk if leaked. Migration involves ensuring applications use projected (bound) tokens instead, then deleting the legacy Secret-based tokens. The bound tokens auto-expire and are invalidated when their Pod is deleted.

questions.question: What does the RBAC verb 'escalate' allow, and why is it dangerous?
options: It allows scaling Deployments to more replicas
It allows a user to grant permissions they do not have themselves, bypassing RBAC's normal escalation prevention
It allows elevating Pod priority
It allows promoting a user from developer to administrator
questions.explanation: The 'escalate' verb on Roles/ClusterRoles allows a user to create or modify roles with permissions they do not hold themselves. Normally, RBAC prevents privilege escalation by blocking users from granting permissions they lack. The 'escalate' verb bypasses this protection and should be granted extremely carefully.

questions.question: Which API server flag restricts API access to only allow specific authentication methods?
options: --anonymous-auth=false
--enable-admission-plugins
--authorization-mode
--client-ca-file (to require client certificate authentication)
questions.explanation: Setting --anonymous-auth=false ensures that all API requests must be authenticated. Combined with configuring specific authentication methods (--client-ca-file for certificates, --oidc-issuer-url for OIDC, etc.), this ensures only verified identities can access the API.

questions.question: You need to test whether a ServiceAccount has specific permissions without actually performing the action. Which kubectl command helps?
options: kubectl describe sa <name>
kubectl auth can-i <verb> <resource> --as=system:serviceaccount:<namespace>:<name>
kubectl get rolebinding -o yaml
kubectl api-resources --namespaced
questions.explanation: kubectl auth can-i with --as flag impersonates a ServiceAccount to check whether it has a specific permission. For example, 'kubectl auth can-i create pods --as=system:serviceaccount:default:my-sa' returns yes/no without actually creating a Pod. This is essential for RBAC auditing.

questions.question: What are the security implications of a ServiceAccount that can 'get' Secrets in its namespace?
options: No security implications; reading Secrets is a normal operation
The ServiceAccount can read all Secret data in the namespace, including tokens, passwords, and certificates used by other applications
The ServiceAccount can only read its own token Secret
Get on Secrets only returns Secret metadata, not the data
questions.explanation: The 'get' verb on Secrets allows reading the full Secret data (including base64-decoded values) for ALL Secrets in the namespace. This includes database passwords, API keys, TLS certificates, and other ServiceAccount tokens. Secret access should be scoped to specific Secret names using resourceNames when possible.

questions.question: What is the principle of least privilege in the context of Kubernetes RBAC?
options: Grant every user cluster-admin access for convenience
Grant only the minimum permissions required for a user or ServiceAccount to perform their specific function
Use only ClusterRoles instead of namespace-scoped Roles
Disable RBAC and use ABAC instead
questions.explanation: Least privilege means granting only the minimum set of permissions needed. For example, a deployment pipeline ServiceAccount might need create/update on Deployments but not delete, and should be scoped to specific namespaces rather than cluster-wide.

questions.question: How does the 'bind' RBAC verb differ from 'escalate', and when is it needed?
options: They are identical in function
'bind' allows creating RoleBindings/ClusterRoleBindings that reference a specific Role; it is needed when users must bind Roles they do not fully hold
'bind' is used for Pod-to-Service binding
'bind' allows binding PersistentVolumes to PersistentVolumeClaims
questions.explanation: The 'bind' verb on Roles/ClusterRoles allows creating RoleBindings that reference a specific Role. Unlike 'escalate' (which lets you modify Role rules), 'bind' only lets you assign an existing Role to subjects. This is a narrower permission -- users can bind a pre-defined Role but cannot change what the Role allows.

questions.question: What should you verify about RBAC after a Kubernetes version upgrade?
options: That all Pods have been restarted
That default ClusterRoles and ClusterRoleBindings have not been modified in ways that expand permissions
That the RBAC API version is still supported
That all users have changed their passwords
questions.explanation: Kubernetes upgrades can modify default ClusterRoles (like system:discovery, system:basic-user). After an upgrade, verify that these default roles have not gained new permissions that conflict with your security policy. Also check that deprecated RBAC patterns are updated.

questions.question: Why should Pods in the kube-system namespace have carefully scoped ServiceAccount permissions?
options: kube-system Pods have no access to the API server
kube-system Pods often run with elevated privileges; a compromised kube-system Pod could affect cluster-wide components
kube-system Pods are not affected by RBAC
kube-system namespace has its own authentication system
questions.explanation: kube-system Pods run critical cluster components (CoreDNS, kube-proxy, controllers). They often have elevated RBAC permissions. A compromised kube-system Pod could modify DNS resolution, network rules, or other cluster-wide settings. Each component should have a dedicated ServiceAccount with only the permissions it needs.

questions.question: An organization uses an external OIDC provider for Kubernetes authentication. What additional security measure should be configured?
options: Disable all other authentication methods
Configure --oidc-required-claim to restrict access to users with specific claims (e.g., group membership)
Allow all OIDC users cluster-admin access
Store OIDC tokens as Kubernetes Secrets
questions.explanation: --oidc-required-claim ensures only OIDC tokens with specific claims are accepted. For example, requiring a 'department=engineering' claim restricts API access to users who belong to the engineering department, even if they have valid OIDC tokens. This provides an additional layer beyond RBAC.

questions.question: What is the danger of granting 'create' permission on Pods to a ServiceAccount without admission controller restrictions?
options: The ServiceAccount can only create Pods in its own namespace
The ServiceAccount can create privileged Pods, mount host filesystems, or use host networking to escape the container boundary
Pod creation is always safe regardless of RBAC
Pods created by ServiceAccounts cannot access the network
questions.explanation: Pod creation with RBAC alone does not restrict the Pod's security context. Without admission controllers (like PodSecurity), a ServiceAccount with Pod creation rights can specify privileged: true, hostNetwork: true, and hostPath volumes, effectively gaining root access to the node.

questions.question: What is AppArmor in the context of Kubernetes security?
options: A Kubernetes admission controller
A Linux kernel security module that restricts programs to a limited set of resources using profiles
A container image scanning tool
A network encryption protocol
questions.explanation: AppArmor is a Linux kernel security module that confines programs to a limited set of resources. In Kubernetes, AppArmor profiles can be applied to containers to restrict file access, network operations, and system capabilities beyond what SecurityContext provides.

questions.question: How is an AppArmor profile applied to a container in Kubernetes?
options: By adding an annotation: container.apparmor.security.beta.kubernetes.io/<container-name>: localhost/<profile-name>
By setting appArmor: true in the SecurityContext
By creating a ConfigMap with the AppArmor profile
By installing AppArmor on the Kubernetes control plane
questions.explanation: AppArmor profiles are applied via Pod annotations in the format container.apparmor.security.beta.kubernetes.io/<container-name>: localhost/<profile-name>. The profile must be pre-loaded on the node where the Pod runs. Newer Kubernetes versions also support securityContext.appArmorProfile.

questions.question: What is a seccomp profile?
options: A security configuration for the Kubernetes scheduler
A Linux kernel feature that filters which system calls a process can make
A network security policy for container communication
A tool for encrypting container images
questions.explanation: Seccomp (Secure Computing Mode) is a Linux kernel feature that restricts the system calls a process can make. In Kubernetes, seccomp profiles whitelist which syscalls are allowed, blocking potentially dangerous calls like mount, ptrace, or reboot. This significantly reduces the kernel attack surface.

questions.question: What is the 'RuntimeDefault' seccomp profile?
options: A profile that blocks all system calls
The container runtime's default seccomp profile that blocks known dangerous syscalls while allowing common ones
A profile that allows all system calls
A profile specific to each Kubernetes version
questions.explanation: RuntimeDefault uses the container runtime's built-in seccomp profile (e.g., Docker's default). It blocks dangerous syscalls like clock_settime, reboot, and mount while allowing the ~300+ syscalls that most applications need. It is a good starting point before creating custom profiles.

questions.question: A custom seccomp profile uses 'defaultAction: SCMP_ACT_ERRNO' and includes a whitelist of allowed syscalls. What happens when the process makes a syscall not on the whitelist?
options: The syscall succeeds but is logged
The process is immediately killed
The syscall returns an error (EPERM) to the calling process
The syscall is forwarded to the host kernel unfiltered
questions.explanation: SCMP_ACT_ERRNO is the default action for syscalls not in the whitelist. It returns a permission error to the process rather than killing it. SCMP_ACT_KILL_PROCESS would kill the process, and SCMP_ACT_LOG would allow but log the syscall. Using ERRNO allows applications to handle the denial gracefully.

questions.question: What does 'drop: [ALL]' in a container's SecurityContext capabilities section do?
options: Drops all containers from the Pod
Removes all Linux capabilities from the container process
Deletes all files in the container filesystem
Disconnects the container from all networks
questions.explanation: Dropping ALL capabilities removes every Linux capability from the container process, including common ones like NET_RAW and SYS_CHROOT. Specific capabilities can then be added back selectively. This follows the principle of least privilege by starting with zero capabilities and adding only what is needed.

questions.question: Which Linux capability must be explicitly added back if you drop ALL capabilities and need the container to bind to ports below 1024?
options: SYS_ADMIN
NET_BIND_SERVICE
NET_RAW
DAC_OVERRIDE
questions.explanation: NET_BIND_SERVICE allows a process to bind to privileged ports (below 1024) without running as root. After dropping ALL capabilities, you must add it back if the application needs to listen on ports like 80 or 443. SYS_ADMIN should almost never be added as it grants near-root privileges.

questions.question: Why should you disable unused kernel modules on Kubernetes worker nodes?
options: To free up disk space
To reduce the kernel attack surface by removing code that could contain exploitable vulnerabilities
To improve Pod scheduling performance
To enable container image scanning
questions.explanation: Unused kernel modules (like USB drivers or uncommon filesystem types) expand the kernel attack surface. Container escape exploits often target kernel vulnerabilities. Disabling unneeded modules with modprobe blacklisting or removing them reduces the attack surface available to compromised containers.

questions.question: What is the security benefit of running a read-only root filesystem in a container?
options: It improves container startup time
It prevents attackers from writing malicious scripts, backdoors, or modifying existing binaries in the container
It encrypts the filesystem contents
It prevents the container from reading sensitive files
questions.explanation: A read-only root filesystem prevents any writes to the container's filesystem. Attackers cannot write malware, modify configuration files, or create reverse shell scripts. Applications that need writable directories (like /tmp) can use emptyDir volumes mounted at those paths.

questions.question: Why should SSH be disabled on Kubernetes worker nodes?
options: SSH consumes too much CPU
SSH provides an alternative entry point that bypasses Kubernetes RBAC and audit logging
SSH is incompatible with container runtimes
SSH prevents Pod scheduling
questions.explanation: SSH on worker nodes provides direct host access that bypasses Kubernetes access controls and audit logging. An attacker with SSH access can manipulate containers, read secrets from the kubelet, and modify node configuration. Node access should be through kubectl debug or a bastion host with proper audit trails.

questions.question: An organization minimizes the host OS footprint on worker nodes. Which approach best achieves this?
options: Install a full Ubuntu Server with all development tools for debugging
Use a minimal, container-optimized OS (like Bottlerocket, Talos, or Flatcar) with an immutable filesystem
Use a standard Linux distribution but disable the firewall for performance
Install only Kubernetes components on a Windows Server
questions.explanation: Container-optimized OSes like Bottlerocket, Talos, and Flatcar are purpose-built for running containers. They have minimal packages (no SSH, no package manager), immutable filesystems, automatic updates, and reduced attack surface. This minimizes the host OS footprint significantly compared to general-purpose Linux distributions.

questions.question: A container needs the SYS_PTRACE capability. Why is this a security concern, and when might it be justified?
options: SYS_PTRACE allows network packet capture
SYS_PTRACE allows tracing and debugging other processes, potentially enabling container escape or credential theft from other processes in the same PID namespace
SYS_PTRACE allows mounting external filesystems
SYS_PTRACE has no security implications
questions.explanation: SYS_PTRACE allows a process to attach to and debug other processes using ptrace(). In shared PID namespaces, this could enable reading memory of other containers' processes (stealing credentials, encryption keys). It is justified for debugging sidecars or profiling tools but should be used sparingly with tight RBAC controls.

questions.question: How do you create a custom AppArmor profile for a container that only needs to read files from /data and write to /tmp?
options: Use a predefined profile; custom profiles are not supported
Write a profile that denies all file access except read on /data/** and read-write on /tmp/**, load it on the node, then reference it in the Pod annotation
Set readOnlyRootFilesystem: true; that is equivalent to AppArmor
Configure a NetworkPolicy to restrict file access
questions.explanation: Custom AppArmor profiles define fine-grained file, network, and capability restrictions. You write a profile with specific path rules (e.g., '/data/** r,' and '/tmp/** rw,'), load it on all nodes where the Pod may run using apparmor_parser, then reference it via the Pod annotation. This is more granular than SecurityContext alone.

questions.question: How do you apply a seccomp profile to a Pod in Kubernetes?
options: Using the securityContext.seccompProfile field in the Pod or container spec
Using a ConfigMap mounted at /etc/seccomp
Using a Kubernetes annotation: seccomp.kubernetes.io/pod
Using an environment variable: SECCOMP_PROFILE
questions.explanation: The securityContext.seccompProfile field (GA since Kubernetes 1.19) sets the seccomp profile. type: RuntimeDefault uses the runtime's default, type: Localhost with localhostProfile: <path> uses a custom profile from the node's seccomp profile directory (usually /var/lib/kubelet/seccomp/).

questions.question: Why should the container runtime version be kept up to date on worker nodes?
options: To support the latest Kubernetes Dashboard features
To patch container escape vulnerabilities and privilege escalation bugs in the runtime
To improve container image download speeds
To enable IPv6 support
questions.explanation: Container runtime vulnerabilities (like CVE-2019-5736 in runc or CVE-2024-21626) can enable container escape to the host. Keeping the runtime (containerd, CRI-O) and its dependencies (runc) updated patches these critical security vulnerabilities. Runtime updates should be tested and applied promptly.

questions.question: What is the security purpose of using a minimal base image (like distroless or Alpine) for containers?
options: Minimal images are automatically encrypted
They reduce the number of exploitable packages, tools, and shells available to an attacker after a container compromise
They provide built-in malware scanning
They enforce network isolation between containers
questions.explanation: Minimal base images remove package managers, shells (bash, sh), debugging tools, and unnecessary libraries. If an attacker gains code execution in the container, they have fewer tools available for lateral movement, data exfiltration, or further exploitation. Distroless images do not even include a shell.

questions.question: What does the SYS_ADMIN capability allow, and why is it considered almost equivalent to root?
options: It allows administering user accounts only
It grants a wide range of privileged operations including mount, unmount, setting hostname, and configuring kernel parameters
It allows managing Kubernetes RBAC roles
It only allows administering the container's own processes
questions.explanation: SYS_ADMIN is a catch-all capability that grants numerous privileged operations: mounting filesystems, using unshare() for namespace creation, performing BPF operations, and more. It is frequently used in container escape exploits. The CIS Benchmark and Restricted Pod Security Standard both prohibit it.

questions.question: A node's /etc/fstab mounts the kubelet data directory with default options. What security improvement can be made?
options: Mount with nosuid and noexec options to prevent setuid binaries and script execution from volume data
Change the kubelet data directory to /tmp
Remove the fstab entry entirely
Mount with read-only options
questions.explanation: Mounting the kubelet data directory (where container volumes are stored) with nosuid and noexec prevents setuid binaries from escalating privileges and prevents direct execution of files from volumes. This is a defense-in-depth measure against attacks that write and execute malicious binaries via mounted volumes.

questions.question: What are the three AppArmor profile modes?
options: Allow, Deny, Audit
Enforce, Complain, Unconfined
Strict, Moderate, Permissive
Block, Warn, Ignore
questions.explanation: AppArmor profiles operate in three modes: Enforce (violations are blocked and logged), Complain (violations are logged but allowed -- useful for profile development), and Unconfined (no restrictions). Production containers should use Enforce mode, while Complain mode helps develop and test profiles before enforcement.

questions.question: How does minimizing IAM roles for Kubernetes nodes improve cluster security?
options: It reduces the number of nodes in the cluster
It limits the blast radius if a node is compromised, preventing access to cloud resources beyond what the node needs
It improves node boot time
It disables RBAC on the node
questions.explanation: Cloud IAM roles assigned to nodes determine what cloud resources (S3 buckets, databases, other services) the node can access. Over-permissive IAM roles mean a compromised container (via instance metadata service) can access cloud resources it should not. Roles should follow least privilege, granting only necessary permissions.

questions.question: You want to generate a custom seccomp profile based on an application's actual syscall usage. Which approach is recommended?
options: Manually write the profile by reading the application's source code
Run the application with seccomp in log mode (SCMP_ACT_LOG) or use tools like 'strace' or the Security Profiles Operator to record actual syscalls, then create a whitelist-based profile
Copy the RuntimeDefault profile and use it as-is
Disable seccomp and rely on AppArmor instead
questions.explanation: The best approach is empirical: run the application and record the actual syscalls it makes. Tools like the Security Profiles Operator (SPO) can record and generate profiles. Running with SCMP_ACT_LOG or using strace captures the syscall list. This ensures the profile allows all needed syscalls while blocking everything else.

questions.question: What is the security risk of running containers with 'hostPID: true'?
options: The container can see other containers' network traffic
The container shares the host's PID namespace, allowing it to see and potentially interact with all processes on the node including other containers
The container's processes get the highest scheduling priority
The container can modify the host's DNS settings
questions.explanation: hostPID: true shares the host's process namespace with the container. The container can see all processes running on the node, including other containers' processes. Combined with SYS_PTRACE capability, an attacker could read memory of other processes, inject code, or steal credentials.

questions.question: What is the Pod Security Admission controller?
options: A tool for scanning container images
A built-in admission controller that enforces Pod Security Standards at the namespace level
A network firewall for Pod traffic
A scheduler plugin for Pod placement
questions.explanation: The Pod Security Admission (PSA) controller is built into Kubernetes (replacing PodSecurityPolicy) and enforces Pod Security Standards (Privileged, Baseline, Restricted) at the namespace level. It validates Pod specs against the configured standard and can enforce (reject), audit (log), or warn (show warnings).

questions.question: What are the three enforcement modes for Pod Security Admission?
options: Allow, Deny, Log
Enforce, Audit, Warn
Block, Monitor, Ignore
Strict, Standard, Permissive
questions.explanation: Pod Security Admission has three modes: Enforce (reject Pods that violate the standard), Audit (allow but log violations in the audit log), and Warn (allow but display a warning to the user). These can be set independently, allowing gradual migration -- e.g., warn on Restricted while enforcing Baseline.

questions.question: A namespace has the label 'pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted'. A developer tries to create a Pod without 'runAsNonRoot: true'. What happens?
options: The Pod is created with a warning
The Pod is created but logged in the audit log
The Pod creation is rejected by the admission controller
The Pod is created but cannot be scheduled
questions.explanation: With enforce mode set to 'restricted', Pods that do not comply with the Restricted standard are rejected. The Restricted standard requires runAsNonRoot: true, dropping ALL capabilities, a seccomp profile, and no privilege escalation. The developer must fix the Pod spec before it can be created.

questions.question: What is OPA Gatekeeper in the context of Kubernetes?
options: A network gateway for inter-cluster communication
A policy engine that uses custom constraint templates to enforce policies on Kubernetes resources via admission control
A Kubernetes authentication provider
A container image registry
questions.explanation: OPA Gatekeeper is a validating admission webhook that uses the Open Policy Agent (OPA) engine. It allows writing custom ConstraintTemplates in Rego to enforce organization-specific policies -- like requiring resource limits, specific labels, trusted image registries, or blocking privileged containers.

questions.question: How does OPA Gatekeeper differ from Pod Security Admission?
options: They are identical in functionality
Gatekeeper supports custom policies on any Kubernetes resource, while PSA only enforces predefined Pod Security Standards on Pods
PSA is more customizable than Gatekeeper
Gatekeeper only works with container images, not Pods
questions.explanation: Pod Security Admission is limited to enforcing the three predefined Pod Security Standards on Pod resources. Gatekeeper can enforce custom policies on any Kubernetes resource type (Deployments, Services, Ingresses, etc.) using Rego policies. Gatekeeper is more flexible but requires maintaining custom policy code.

questions.question: You need to enforce that all Pods must have resource limits defined. Which approach is most robust?
options: Create a LimitRange with defaults in every namespace
Use a Gatekeeper ConstraintTemplate that rejects Pods without resource limits, combined with a LimitRange as a fallback
Add a comment in the deployment documentation requesting developers to add limits
Run a weekly audit script that reports non-compliant Pods
questions.explanation: A layered approach is most robust: Gatekeeper rejects Pods without explicit resource limits (enforcement), while LimitRange provides defaults as a safety net if Gatekeeper is bypassed. Documentation and auditing are useful but do not prevent non-compliant resources from being created.

questions.question: How are Kubernetes Secrets stored in etcd by default?
options: Encrypted with AES-256-GCM
Encrypted with the cluster's TLS certificate
Base64-encoded but NOT encrypted
Stored as plaintext
questions.explanation: By default, Kubernetes Secrets are stored in etcd as base64-encoded data, which is NOT encryption. Anyone with access to etcd (either through the API or direct etcd access) can read Secret values. Encryption at rest must be explicitly configured using EncryptionConfiguration.

questions.question: How do you enable encryption at rest for Kubernetes Secrets in etcd?
options: Set --encrypt-secrets=true on the API server
Create an EncryptionConfiguration file specifying encryption providers and configure the API server with --encryption-provider-config
Enable TLS on etcd
Use a third-party secrets manager instead of Kubernetes Secrets
questions.explanation: Encryption at rest requires creating an EncryptionConfiguration YAML file that specifies which resources to encrypt and which encryption provider to use (aescbc, aesgcm, secretbox, or kms). The API server is configured with --encryption-provider-config pointing to this file. Existing Secrets must be re-encrypted after enabling.

questions.question: After enabling encryption at rest, existing Secrets are still stored unencrypted. How do you encrypt them?
options: Restart the API server; it re-encrypts automatically
Run 'kubectl get secrets --all-namespaces -o json | kubectl replace -f -' to trigger re-encryption on write
Delete and recreate all Secrets
Run 'etcdctl encrypt --all'
questions.explanation: Existing Secrets remain in their original format until they are rewritten. Reading all Secrets and replacing them triggers the API server to re-encrypt them with the new provider on write. This is the standard approach documented by Kubernetes for encrypting pre-existing data.

questions.question: What is a container runtime sandbox?
options: A staging environment for testing container images
An additional isolation layer between the container and the host kernel, providing stronger security boundaries
A development tool for writing Dockerfiles
A network sandbox that isolates container DNS
questions.explanation: Container runtime sandboxes (like gVisor and Kata Containers) add an extra isolation layer between the container and the host kernel. gVisor intercepts syscalls in user space, while Kata Containers run each Pod in a lightweight VM. Both reduce the risk of container escape exploits targeting kernel vulnerabilities.

questions.question: How does gVisor provide container isolation?
options: By running containers in virtual machines
By intercepting container syscalls in a user-space kernel (Sentry) that implements a subset of Linux syscalls without passing them to the host kernel
By encrypting all container network traffic
By restricting container CPU usage
questions.explanation: gVisor runs a user-space kernel called Sentry that intercepts syscalls from the container application. The Sentry implements Linux syscalls independently without forwarding them to the host kernel (except for a small set handled by Gofer for filesystem operations). This dramatically reduces the host kernel attack surface.

questions.question: How do you configure a Pod to use a specific container runtime sandbox like gVisor?
options: Set 'sandbox: gvisor' in the SecurityContext
Create a RuntimeClass resource and reference it in the Pod's runtimeClassName field
Install gVisor inside the container image
Set the container runtime to gVisor in the kubelet configuration for all Pods
questions.explanation: RuntimeClass is a Kubernetes resource that maps a name to a specific container runtime handler (e.g., runsc for gVisor, kata for Kata Containers). You create a RuntimeClass pointing to the handler, then set spec.runtimeClassName in the Pod spec. This allows per-Pod runtime selection.

questions.question: What is the role of mTLS (mutual TLS) in a service mesh like Istio?
options: It encrypts data at rest in etcd
It provides mutual authentication and encryption between services, ensuring both client and server verify each other's identity
It authenticates users to the Kubernetes Dashboard
It encrypts container images during pull
questions.explanation: mTLS in a service mesh encrypts all service-to-service traffic and provides mutual authentication -- both the client and server present certificates and verify each other's identity. This prevents eavesdropping, man-in-the-middle attacks, and unauthorized service communication within the cluster.

questions.question: An organization enables Istio strict mTLS mode. What happens to a Pod that is not part of the service mesh trying to communicate with a mesh-enabled Service?
options: The communication succeeds because Istio is transparent
The connection is rejected because the non-mesh Pod cannot present a valid mTLS certificate
The traffic is automatically encrypted by the CNI plugin
The Pod is automatically injected with the Istio sidecar
questions.explanation: In strict mTLS mode, Istio requires all incoming connections to present valid mTLS certificates issued by the mesh's CA. A Pod outside the mesh does not have the Istio sidecar proxy and cannot present a valid certificate, so its connections are rejected. Permissive mode would allow both mTLS and plaintext.

questions.question: What is the Vault CSI Provider used for in Kubernetes?
options: To store container images in HashiCorp Vault
To inject secrets from HashiCorp Vault into Pods as mounted files using the CSI Secrets Store Driver
To encrypt Kubernetes Secrets using Vault's transit engine
To authenticate Kubernetes users against Vault policies
questions.explanation: The Vault CSI Provider works with the Secrets Store CSI Driver to mount secrets from HashiCorp Vault directly into Pods as files. This avoids storing sensitive data in Kubernetes Secrets and leverages Vault's access policies, audit logging, and dynamic secret generation.

questions.question: Which Pod Security Standard level should be used for system-level workloads like CNI plugins or monitoring agents that require host-level access?
options: Restricted
Baseline
Privileged
Custom
questions.explanation: The Privileged standard is completely unrestricted and is intended for system-level workloads that genuinely require host-level access (CNI plugins, storage drivers, monitoring agents). These workloads typically need hostNetwork, hostPID, privileged containers, or hostPath volumes. Privileged should only be used for infrastructure namespaces.

questions.question: What encryption provider is recommended for production Kubernetes Secret encryption at rest?
options: identity (no encryption)
aescbc with a static key
KMS (Key Management Service) provider using a cloud KMS
Base64 encoding
questions.explanation: The KMS provider is recommended for production because it uses a cloud-managed KMS (AWS KMS, GCP KMS, Azure Key Vault) for key management. The encryption key is never stored on disk in the API server. aescbc works but the key is stored locally in the EncryptionConfiguration file, which must be separately secured.

questions.question: Why should sensitive configuration values be stored in Secrets rather than ConfigMaps?
options: Secrets are automatically encrypted in transit
Secrets have additional access controls, can be encrypted at rest, and tools understand to treat them as sensitive data
ConfigMaps cannot store string values
Secrets are stored on disk while ConfigMaps are in memory
questions.explanation: While Secrets and ConfigMaps are structurally similar, Secrets have additional security features: they can be encrypted at rest, RBAC can be configured to restrict Secret access separately from ConfigMaps, and Kubernetes tools treat them as sensitive (e.g., kubectl get secret does not display data by default).

questions.question: A Gatekeeper ConstraintTemplate uses the Rego rule: 'violation[{"msg": msg}] { not input.review.object.spec.template.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot; msg := "Pods must run as non-root" }'. What does this enforce?
options: All Services must have a non-root selector
All Pod templates must have runAsNonRoot set to true in the Pod-level SecurityContext
All nodes must run non-root processes
All container images must be built with a non-root USER
questions.explanation: This Rego rule checks if the Pod template's securityContext has runAsNonRoot set. If it is not set (or false), a violation is generated with the message 'Pods must run as non-root'. Gatekeeper rejects the resource creation if any violations exist. Note this checks the spec template, so it catches Deployments, not just bare Pods.

questions.question: How does a service mesh help detect and prevent unauthorized service-to-service communication?
options: By encrypting DNS queries
By enforcing authorization policies that define which services can communicate with each other, beyond what NetworkPolicies offer
By scanning container images for malware
By monitoring CPU usage of each service
questions.explanation: Service meshes enforce L7 authorization policies (e.g., Istio AuthorizationPolicy) that control which services can call which endpoints, including HTTP method and path restrictions. This is more granular than NetworkPolicies (L3/L4). Combined with mTLS identity, it prevents impersonation and unauthorized access.

questions.question: What is the trade-off of using gVisor for container sandboxing?
options: No trade-offs; gVisor is always better than standard runc
Stronger isolation at the cost of performance overhead and potential incompatibility with some syscalls
Better performance but weaker isolation
gVisor requires more memory but has no CPU overhead
questions.explanation: gVisor provides stronger isolation by intercepting syscalls in user space, but this adds performance overhead (especially for I/O-intensive and syscall-heavy workloads). Some applications may encounter incompatibilities because gVisor does not implement every Linux syscall. It is best suited for untrusted or multi-tenant workloads where security outweighs performance.

questions.question: Which Pod Security Standard prohibits containers from running as root?
options: Privileged
Baseline
Restricted
Both Baseline and Restricted
questions.explanation: Only the Restricted standard requires runAsNonRoot: true. The Baseline standard prevents known privilege escalations (privileged containers, hostPath, etc.) but does not require running as non-root. Privileged has no restrictions at all.

questions.question: An application needs database credentials that rotate every 24 hours. What is the most secure approach in Kubernetes?
options: Store the credentials in a ConfigMap and update it daily
Use a dynamic secret engine (like Vault's database engine) that generates short-lived credentials on demand for each Pod
Store the credentials in a Kubernetes Secret and manually update it daily
Hardcode the credentials in the container image and rebuild daily
questions.explanation: Dynamic secret engines generate unique, short-lived credentials for each consumer. Each Pod gets its own database credentials that auto-expire. This eliminates shared credentials, enables audit trails per consumer, and automatic revocation when Pods are deleted. This is significantly more secure than rotating static credentials.

questions.question: The EncryptionConfiguration lists two providers: 'aescbc' first, then 'identity'. What does this configuration mean?
options: All operations use aescbc encryption; identity is ignored
New Secrets are written using aescbc (first provider), but existing Secrets can be read with either provider (used during migration)
Reads use aescbc; writes use identity
The configuration is invalid; only one provider is allowed
questions.explanation: The first provider in the list is used for encryption on writes. All listed providers are tried in order for decryption on reads. Listing 'identity' (no encryption) as a read provider allows the API server to read old unencrypted Secrets while writing new ones encrypted with aescbc. This is the standard migration pattern.

questions.question: What is the primary security benefit of a service mesh in a Kubernetes cluster?
options: Faster container image pulls
Automatic encryption and authentication of all service-to-service traffic without application code changes
Reduced cluster resource usage
Automatic vulnerability scanning of running containers
questions.explanation: A service mesh provides transparent mTLS encryption and mutual authentication for all service-to-service communication. This is injected via sidecar proxies without requiring application code changes, ensuring that even legacy applications benefit from encrypted, authenticated communication.

questions.question: What is a ValidatingWebhookConfiguration in Kubernetes?
options: A webhook that modifies incoming API requests
A webhook that validates incoming API requests and can accept or reject them based on custom logic
A webhook that authenticates users
A webhook that monitors Pod health
questions.explanation: ValidatingWebhookConfiguration defines a webhook server that validates API requests. When a resource is created or modified, the API server sends the request to the webhook, which returns an allow or deny decision. OPA Gatekeeper uses this mechanism. MutatingWebhookConfiguration modifies requests instead of just validating them.

questions.question: A team wants to gradually adopt the Restricted Pod Security Standard. What is the recommended migration approach?
options: Enable enforce: restricted immediately and fix all failures
Set warn: restricted and audit: restricted first to identify non-compliant Pods, then fix violations before enabling enforce: restricted
Only apply Restricted to new namespaces, never to existing ones
Run kube-bench to check compliance
questions.explanation: The recommended migration is gradual: first set warn and audit modes to Restricted to identify all non-compliant Pods without blocking them. Fix violations across workloads, then enable enforce mode. This prevents production disruptions while systematically improving security posture.

questions.question: Kata Containers provide isolation by running Pods inside lightweight VMs. What is the key advantage over gVisor?
options: Kata is faster than gVisor for all workloads
Kata provides hardware-level isolation with a separate kernel per Pod, offering stronger isolation for workloads requiring full kernel compatibility
Kata does not require any special node configuration
Kata supports more container orchestrators than gVisor
questions.explanation: Kata Containers run each Pod in a lightweight VM with its own kernel, providing hardware-level isolation. This is stronger than gVisor's user-space kernel approach and offers full Linux kernel compatibility (all syscalls work). The trade-off is higher resource overhead (each Pod needs VM resources) and slightly longer startup time.

questions.question: Why should Kubernetes Secrets not be used in environment variables for sensitive data?
options: Environment variables cannot hold Secret data
Environment variables may appear in logs, crash dumps, and are visible via the Kubernetes API and container inspection, increasing exposure risk
Environment variables are slower than volume mounts
Environment variables cannot be updated after Pod creation
questions.explanation: Environment variables with Secret values can appear in application logs (accidental logging), crash dumps, and are visible via 'kubectl describe pod' and the API. Volume-mounted Secrets are more secure because they are files on disk (not in the process environment) and can be restricted with filesystem permissions.

questions.question: What is the risk of using etcd without encryption at rest in a Kubernetes cluster?
options: etcd performance degrades without encryption
An attacker with access to etcd storage (disk or backup) can read all cluster Secrets, tokens, and configuration data in plaintext
etcd cannot replicate without encryption
Pods cannot start without etcd encryption
questions.explanation: Without encryption at rest, all Kubernetes data in etcd (including Secrets, ServiceAccount tokens, and configuration) is stored in plaintext (or base64). An attacker who gains access to etcd backups, disk snapshots, or the etcd data directory can read everything. This is especially dangerous in cloud environments where disk snapshots may be accessible.

questions.question: Why should container images use minimal base images (like distroless) for production?
options: To reduce image download time only
To minimize the attack surface by removing unnecessary packages, shells, and tools that attackers could exploit
To comply with Kubernetes version requirements
To enable multi-architecture builds
questions.explanation: Minimal base images reduce the attack surface by removing shells (bash, sh), package managers (apt, apk), debugging tools, and unnecessary libraries. Fewer packages mean fewer potential CVEs and fewer tools available to an attacker after a container compromise. Google's distroless images contain only the application and its runtime dependencies.

questions.question: What is the difference between Alpine-based and distroless base images from a security perspective?
options: They are equally secure
Alpine includes a package manager (apk) and shell (ash); distroless includes neither, providing a smaller attack surface
Distroless images are larger because they include more security tools
Alpine is more secure because it uses musl libc instead of glibc
questions.explanation: Alpine includes apk (package manager), ash (shell), and basic utilities. While small, these tools could be used by an attacker. Distroless images contain ONLY the application runtime (Java, Python, Node, etc.) with no shell or package manager, making post-exploitation significantly harder.

questions.question: What is the purpose of container image scanning in a CI/CD pipeline?
options: To optimize image size
To detect known vulnerabilities (CVEs) in image packages and dependencies before deployment
To verify the image runs correctly
To compress image layers
questions.explanation: Image scanning tools (Trivy, Grype, Snyk) analyze container images for known CVEs in OS packages, application dependencies, and configuration issues. Integrating scanning into CI/CD catches vulnerabilities before images reach production, preventing deployment of images with critical security flaws.

questions.question: Which open-source tool is commonly used for container image vulnerability scanning in Kubernetes environments?
options: kube-hunter
Trivy
kube-bench
Falco
questions.explanation: Trivy is an open-source vulnerability scanner that scans container images, filesystems, and Kubernetes configurations for CVEs. It maintains an updated vulnerability database and can be integrated into CI/CD pipelines. It scans OS packages, language-specific dependencies, and IaC configurations.

questions.question: Trivy reports a HIGH severity CVE in a base image package. The package is not used by the application. What is the recommended approach?
options: Ignore all HIGH CVEs that are in base image packages
Assess the CVE: if the package is truly unused and unexploitable, document the exception, but still update the base image or use a more minimal image to reduce future risk
Switch to a different scanner that does not report it
Mark all Trivy findings as false positives
questions.explanation: Even if a CVE is in an unused package, it still increases attack surface. The recommended approach is: assess exploitability, document exceptions with justification, and plan remediation (update base image or switch to a minimal image). Never blindly ignore CVEs -- context-aware triage is essential.

questions.question: What is the purpose of restricting container registries using an admission controller?
options: To speed up image pulls
To ensure only images from trusted, approved registries can be deployed in the cluster
To reduce storage costs
To enable image caching on nodes
questions.explanation: Registry allowlisting ensures that only images from trusted registries (e.g., your private registry or verified public registries) can be deployed. This prevents the use of untrusted or potentially malicious images from arbitrary public registries. OPA Gatekeeper or custom admission webhooks commonly enforce this.

questions.question: How does image signing with Cosign improve supply chain security?
options: It encrypts the image contents so only authorized users can pull them
It provides a cryptographic signature that verifies the image was built by a trusted party and has not been tampered with
It compresses the image to reduce attack surface
It automatically patches vulnerabilities in the image
questions.explanation: Cosign creates cryptographic signatures for container images. When combined with a verification policy (e.g., Kyverno or Connaisseur admission controller), the cluster only accepts images with valid signatures from trusted keys. This prevents deploying tampered images even if the registry is compromised.

questions.question: What is the ImagePolicyWebhook admission controller used for?
options: To set the default imagePullPolicy for all Pods
To call an external webhook that validates container images against custom policies (signing, scanning results) before allowing Pod creation
To cache container images on nodes
To convert image formats between Docker and OCI
questions.explanation: ImagePolicyWebhook calls an external webhook service to validate container images before allowing Pods to be created. The webhook can check image signatures, vulnerability scan results, registry allowlists, or any custom policy. This enables integration with enterprise image governance workflows.

questions.question: What is the security benefit of using multi-stage Docker builds?
options: Multi-stage builds run containers in multiple security zones
They exclude build tools, compilers, and source code from the final image, reducing attack surface and preventing source code exposure
They automatically scan for vulnerabilities between stages
They enable running multiple applications in one container
questions.explanation: Multi-stage builds separate the build environment from the runtime environment. Build tools (gcc, npm, pip), source code, and intermediate files exist only in build stages. The final stage contains only the compiled binary and necessary runtime files, preventing exposure of source code and reducing the number of exploitable packages.

questions.question: Why should Dockerfiles avoid using 'latest' tags for base images?
options: The 'latest' tag is reserved for Kubernetes system images
The 'latest' tag is mutable and can point to different images over time, breaking reproducibility and potentially introducing unexpected vulnerabilities
The 'latest' tag images are always larger
Kubernetes cannot pull images with the 'latest' tag
questions.explanation: The 'latest' tag is a mutable pointer that can change at any time. An image that was safe today may be replaced with one containing vulnerabilities or even malicious code. Using specific version tags or image digests (sha256:...) ensures reproducible, auditable builds and prevents supply chain attacks via tag manipulation.

questions.question: What is static analysis of Kubernetes manifests and why is it important?
options: Analyzing running Pods for performance issues
Scanning Kubernetes YAML/JSON manifests before deployment to detect security misconfigurations like missing security contexts, privileged containers, or exposed secrets
Monitoring cluster metrics in real time
Testing API server response times
questions.explanation: Static analysis tools (Kubesec, Conftest, Checkov, Datree) scan Kubernetes manifests before they are applied to detect security issues: missing resource limits, privileged containers, host volume mounts, missing seccomp profiles, etc. This shifts security left, catching issues before deployment.

questions.question: How does Conftest differ from Kubesec for static analysis?
options: They are identical tools with different names
Conftest allows writing custom policies in Rego (OPA) for any structured data format, while Kubesec uses predefined security scoring rules specific to Kubernetes
Kubesec supports custom policies while Conftest does not
Conftest only works with Helm charts
questions.explanation: Conftest uses OPA's Rego language to define custom policies that can validate any structured data (YAML, JSON, HCL). It is highly flexible. Kubesec provides predefined security scoring specifically for Kubernetes resources with a simpler interface but less customization. Organizations often use both for comprehensive coverage.

questions.question: What is binary authorization and how does it work with container images?
options: It restricts which binaries can run inside a container at runtime
It is a deploy-time security control that requires container images to have attestations (like vulnerability scan results and build provenance) before they can be deployed
It authorizes users to download container images
It converts container images to binary format for faster execution
questions.explanation: Binary authorization (like Google Binary Authorization or Sigstore policy-controller) requires images to have attestations proving they went through required steps (vulnerability scanning, code review, CI pipeline). At deploy time, the admission controller verifies attestations exist and are signed by trusted authorities before allowing the image to run.

questions.question: An organization's Dockerfile installs packages using 'apt-get install -y curl wget netcat'. From a security perspective, what is the concern?
options: These packages consume too much disk space
These networking tools give an attacker post-exploitation capabilities for data exfiltration, reverse shells, and lateral movement if the container is compromised
These packages conflict with Kubernetes networking
apt-get is slower than apk
questions.explanation: Tools like curl, wget, and netcat are frequently used in post-exploitation: curl/wget for downloading additional malware, netcat for reverse shells and lateral movement. If these tools are not needed by the application at runtime, they should not be in the production image. Use multi-stage builds to keep them in build stages only.

questions.question: What is the security advantage of using image digests (sha256:...) instead of tags?
options: Digests are shorter and easier to manage
Digests are immutable content-addressed references that cannot be changed without altering the image, preventing tag manipulation attacks
Digests enable faster image pulls
Digests automatically include vulnerability scan results
questions.explanation: Image digests are SHA-256 hashes of the image manifest. Unlike tags (which are mutable pointers), digests uniquely identify exact image content. An attacker who compromises a registry cannot replace a digested image without changing the digest. This ensures you always deploy exactly the image you tested.

questions.question: A CI/CD pipeline scans images with Trivy. Which scan result should block deployment to production?
options: Any vulnerability of any severity
CRITICAL and HIGH severity vulnerabilities with available fixes
Only CRITICAL vulnerabilities regardless of fix availability
No scan results should block deployment
questions.explanation: A pragmatic policy blocks on CRITICAL and HIGH CVEs that have available fixes (meaning the vulnerability is patchable by updating a package). This balances security with practicality -- unfixable CVEs require a risk assessment rather than blocking. The specific threshold should be defined in the organization's security policy.

questions.question: What does Kubesec analyze when scoring a Kubernetes manifest?
options: Container image layers
Network latency between Pods
Security-relevant fields in Pod specs (SecurityContext, capabilities, volume mounts, service accounts)
RBAC policies across the cluster
questions.explanation: Kubesec scores Kubernetes manifests based on security-relevant fields: SecurityContext settings (runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem), capability drops, resource limits, ServiceAccount configuration, and volume types. It produces a numeric score and lists positive (hardened) and negative (risky) findings.

questions.question: What is Notary/TUF in the context of container image security?
options: A container orchestration tool
A framework for securing software update systems using signed metadata that protects against compromised repositories
A Kubernetes ingress controller with TLS support
A container image compression algorithm
questions.explanation: Notary implements The Update Framework (TUF) for container images. It uses signed metadata with role-based keys (root, targets, snapshot, timestamp) to protect against multiple attack vectors: compromised keys, replay attacks, and mix-and-match attacks. Docker Content Trust uses Notary for image signing.

questions.question: Why should Dockerfiles include a USER instruction for non-root execution?
options: To improve image build speed
To ensure the application runs as a non-root user, limiting the impact of a container compromise
To enable multi-architecture support
To reduce the image size
questions.explanation: Adding 'USER <non-root-user>' in the Dockerfile ensures the application process runs with limited privileges by default. If the container is compromised, the attacker operates as a non-root user with restricted filesystem and kernel access, making privilege escalation harder.

questions.question: What is the risk of using 'COPY . .' in a Dockerfile without a .dockerignore file?
options: The build will fail
Sensitive files (.env, .git, SSH keys, certificates) may be copied into the image and become accessible to anyone who pulls it
The image will be too large to push
Docker will skip the COPY instruction
questions.explanation: Without .dockerignore, 'COPY . .' includes everything in the build context: .env files with secrets, .git directory with commit history, SSH keys, certificates, and other sensitive files. These end up in the image layers and are accessible to anyone who pulls the image. A .dockerignore file should exclude sensitive files and unnecessary directories.

questions.question: How can you integrate vulnerability scanning into the Kubernetes admission control pipeline?
options: Run Trivy on every node as a DaemonSet
Use an admission webhook that queries a scan results database and rejects images that do not meet the vulnerability threshold
Add a scanning step to the kubelet image pull process
Scan images only in the developer's local environment
questions.explanation: An admission webhook (like those provided by Trivy Operator, Anchore, or Snyk) intercepts Pod creation requests and checks if the container images have been scanned and meet vulnerability policies. If an image has unresolved CRITICAL CVEs, the webhook rejects the Pod creation. This enforces scanning compliance at deploy time.

questions.question: What security issues can OPA Conftest detect in Kubernetes manifests that Kubesec cannot?
options: Conftest cannot detect anything Kubesec cannot
Custom organization-specific policies like required labels, naming conventions, specific image registries, and team-specific security requirements
Conftest detects runtime vulnerabilities while Kubesec only does static analysis
Conftest detects network misconfigurations
questions.explanation: Conftest's custom Rego policies can enforce any organization-specific rule: required labels (team, cost-center), naming conventions, specific registry requirements, banned sidecar images, maximum resource limits, required annotations, etc. Kubesec only checks predefined security best practices.

questions.question: An attacker pushes a modified image to a registry using the same tag as a trusted image. Which security control prevents this attack?
options: Image scanning
Image signature verification combined with image digest pinning in deployment manifests
Rate limiting on the registry
RBAC on the Kubernetes API
questions.explanation: Image signature verification ensures only images signed by trusted keys are deployed, catching unsigned or re-signed malicious images. Image digest pinning in manifests ensures the exact image content is deployed regardless of tag changes. Together, they prevent both tag manipulation and unsigned image deployment.

questions.question: What is the purpose of running 'RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y package && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*' in a single Dockerfile layer?
options: To ensure the package manager works correctly
To reduce the image size by removing the package lists in the same layer, and prevent using stale cached package lists
To install multiple packages simultaneously
To avoid network calls during build
questions.explanation: Combining update, install, and cleanup in one RUN instruction creates a single layer. If cleanup were in a separate RUN, the package lists would still exist in the previous layer (Docker layers are additive). This also ensures apt-get update runs every time, preventing stale cache issues.

questions.question: What is the security benefit of using scratch as a base image?
options: Scratch images have built-in security tools
Scratch is a completely empty base image with zero OS packages, providing the absolute minimum attack surface for statically compiled binaries
Scratch images are faster to build
Scratch automatically applies seccomp profiles
questions.explanation: The 'scratch' image is completely empty -- no OS, no shell, no libraries, no package manager. It works only with statically compiled binaries (like Go applications). This provides the smallest possible attack surface: a compromised container has literally nothing to exploit beyond the application binary itself.

questions.question: When should container images be rescanned for vulnerabilities?
options: Only when the image is first built
Regularly (on a schedule) and when new CVEs are published, because new vulnerabilities are discovered in existing packages
Only after a security incident
Never after the initial scan passes
questions.explanation: Vulnerability databases are updated daily. An image that passed scanning last week may now have CRITICAL CVEs discovered in its packages. Regular rescanning (daily or on new CVE advisories) ensures that images running in production are checked against the latest vulnerability data.

questions.question: A Dockerfile contains 'RUN echo "password123" > /app/config.txt' in an early layer, followed by 'RUN rm /app/config.txt' in a later layer. Is the password secure?
options: Yes, because the file was deleted
No, the password still exists in the earlier image layer and can be extracted by inspecting the image history or extracting layers
Yes, because Docker automatically redacts secrets from layers
It depends on the registry configuration
questions.explanation: Docker image layers are additive and immutable. Deleting a file in a later layer does not remove it from the earlier layer where it was created. Anyone with access to the image can extract the earlier layer and read the password. Secrets should never be put in Dockerfiles -- use build secrets or multi-stage builds instead.

questions.question: Which tool can scan Kubernetes cluster configurations (not just manifests) for security issues?
options: Trivy (with its Kubernetes operator mode)
Docker Compose
Helm lint
kubectl version
questions.explanation: Trivy can run in Kubernetes operator mode (Trivy Operator) to continuously scan running workloads, images, and cluster configurations for vulnerabilities and misconfigurations. It checks actual running state, not just manifests, catching runtime drift from the intended configuration.

questions.question: What is the SLSA framework and how does it relate to container supply chain security?
options: A Kubernetes scheduling algorithm
A framework for supply chain integrity that defines levels of build security, from basic source tracking to fully verified and reproducible builds
A container network policy language
